column2: In a whole slide image of focal proliferative-necrotizing IgA nephropathy, you would observe characteristic features such as segmental or focal thickening of the glomerular capillary walls with a "double contour" appearance due to the deposition of immune complexes. There may be areas of necrosis and hemorrhage within the affected segments, leading to a loss of normal architecture. The basement membrane may appear irregular or fragmented, and there can be associated interstitial inflammation and fibrosis. Additionally, there might be infiltration of mononuclear cells, particularly macrophages and lymphocytes, in the glomeruli and surrounding areas, contributing to the inflammatory response.

In a whole slide image of focal proliferative-necrotizing IgA nephropathy, you would observe characteristic features such as segmental or focal thickening of the glomerular capillary walls, often with a "double contour" appearance due to the accumulation of immune complexes and subsequent deposition of fibrin and cellular debris. There may be areas of necrosis within the glomeruli, appearing as pale, eosinophilic regions. The mesangial area is typically enlarged and may contain granular or flocculent deposits that appear as pink or red-staining material. Additionally, there might be evidence of interstitial fibrosis and inflammatory cell infiltration, particularly lymphocytes and plasma cells, distributed in a periglomerular or peritubular pattern. Capillary loops may show signs of hyalinization or occlusion, and the basement membrane may exhibit splitting or thickening.
